explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF is enabled, which causes row count messages to be sent to the client and degrades performance.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF is set while transactions are used, which can leave transactions open or partially applied on runtime errors.
SELECT * retrieves all columns from Sales.Orders, increasing network traffic and risking schema breakage.
SELECT TOP (10) without an ORDER BY clause yields non-deterministic results.
DISTINCT is unnecessary because OrderID is the primary key of Sales.Orders and is already unique.
WITH (NOLOCK) can read uncommitted data, missing rows, or duplicate rows.
Applying YEAR() to the indexed column OrderDate prevents an index seek.
Comparing integer column CustomerID to nvarchar variable @CustomerIdText forces an implicit data type conversion.
OR-based optional parameter filtering prevents effective index usage and accurate cardinality estimates.
LIKE pattern '%urgent%' begins with a wildcard, forcing an index or table scan.
NOT IN with a subquery evaluating a nullable column can yield unexpected empty result sets if any returned value is NULL.
Wrapping CustomerID in ISNULL prevents index seeks on CustomerID.
Performing arithmetic operation + 0 on column OrderID prevents index seek optimization.
Inequality predicate <> prevents index seeking and usually results in index or table scans.
Hard-coded identifier literal 1 is embedded directly in the predicate.
SELECT TOP (1) without an ORDER BY clause yields non-deterministic results.
CROSS JOIN between Sales.Orders and Sales.Customers creates a Cartesian product.
SELECT TOP (1) without an ORDER BY clause yields non-deterministic results.
Comma-separated join syntax between Sales.Orders and Sales.OrderLines is deprecated; use explicit JOIN syntax.
Correlated scalar subquery counting OrderLines executes per outer row in Sales.Orders.
Correlated EXISTS subquery on Sales.OrderLines could be refactored into a set-based join.
The CASE expression is identically repeated in both the SELECT list and ORDER BY clause.
ORDER BY 2 uses column positional ordinal, which is fragile to select-list schema changes.
OFFSET/FETCH pagination ordered by OrderDate lacks a unique tie-breaker, leading to non-deterministic pages.
UNION triggers an expensive duplicate removal operation across mutually exclusive queries; use UNION ALL.
Using COUNT(*) > 0 to verify row existence forces full aggregation instead of short-circuiting with EXISTS.
SELECT @var = column syntax silently assigns non-deterministic values when multiple rows are returned.
Table variable @OrderIds can cause poor cardinality estimation on non-trivial row counts.
SELECT TOP (5) without an ORDER BY clause yields non-deterministic results.
SELECT TOP (5) without an ORDER BY clause yields non-deterministic results.
SELECT ... INTO implicitly creates temp table #ImplicitOrders, holding schema locks and locking tempdb system tables.
SELECT TOP (2) without an ORDER BY clause yields non-deterministic results.
Cursor order_cursor processes rows procedurally row-by-row instead of using set-based operations.
WHILE loop performs row-by-row iterative processing instead of a set-based T-SQL operation.
Building dynamic SQL string @Sql via variable concatenation risks SQL injection and prevents plan reuse.
EXEC (@Sql) executes dynamic SQL without parameterization.
Building dynamic SQL string @Sql via string concatenation prevents execution plan caching.
sp_executesql is invoked without parameterized inputs.
Temporary table #Scratch is explicitly created but not dropped before procedure completion.
CATCH block catches an error without rethrowing (THROW) or raising an error condition.
RAISERROR is legacy syntax; THROW should be used for raising exceptions.
PRINT statement used for diagnostic output can cause client buffering issues.
Business identifier value 1 is hard-coded directly in the query predicate.
Unlabelled date constant '20160101' is embedded directly in query logic.
CONVERT string '01/02/2016' lacks an explicit style parameter, making it sensitive to language settings.
SELECT TOP (1) without an ORDER BY clause yields non-deterministic results.
COALESCE function used in the join condition prevents efficient join and index optimizations.
SELECT TOP (1) without an ORDER BY clause yields non-deterministic results.
Self-join on Sales.Orders reads the same table twice to retrieve data available in a single row.
ORDER BY on wide nvarchar(max) column Comments requires large memory grants and causes wide sort operations.
SUM aggregate scans the entire Sales.OrderLines table without a WHERE filter.
Row-level predicate ol.OrderID > 0 is placed in HAVING instead of WHERE, delaying filter evaluation.
ORDER BY on a single-row scalar aggregate query without GROUP BY is redundant.
MERGE statement has known concurrency issues and execution bugs; standard UPDATE/INSERT should be preferred.
UPDATE sets Comments = Comments, generating unnecessary transaction log overhead and row locks without altering data.
DELETE filters on Quantity = -1 rather than a primary key or unique identifier predicate.
WAITFOR DELAY pauses thread execution synchronously, consuming worker threads.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is executed outside of a BEGIN TRY ... BEGIN CATCH block.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT ON alters session context and bypasses automatic key generation.
Procedure sp_who is invoked without schema qualification.
SET ROWCOUNT 1 modifies session settings without restoring the original state.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories lacks an explicit column target list.
LIKE pattern with leading wildcard '%' forces an index or table scan.
Dynamic SQL concatenated with input variable @CustomerName introduces SQL injection vulnerabilities and prevents plan reuse.
User parameter @CustomerName is directly concatenated into dynamic SQL string without sanitization or parameterization.
System procedure sp_executesql called without schema qualification sys.sp_executesql.
sp_executesql is executed without parameterized input parameters.
UPDATE statement lacks a WHERE clause, updating every row in table #DemoCustomers.
Unlabelled date constant '2016-01-01' embedded directly in predicate.
Filtering predicate on outer-joined table o in WHERE clause implicitly converts the LEFT JOIN into an INNER JOIN.
WHERE DeliveryMethodID = NULL compares column to NULL using equality operator, which always evaluates to UNKNOWN.
Scalar user-defined function dbo.GetCustomerType called in SELECT list causes row-by-row execution.
CATCH block captures error without rethrowing or failing the batch.
CATCH block handles exception within an open transaction without performing a ROLLBACK.
Hardcoded index hint WITH (INDEX = 1) overrides query optimizer plan choices.
Subquery nesting depth exceeds 3 levels; refactor using CTEs or temporary tables.
LIKE pattern '%A%' contains a leading wildcard, preventing index seek operations.
SELECT * from TVF retrieves all columns, risking schema misalignment.
Function dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti is invoked as a multi-statement table-valued function which impedes optimization compared to an inline TVF.
